sections.plain_summary: Gut dysbiosis means the community of microbes living in your digestive tract has become unbalanced — the wrong bacteria, fungi, or other organisms are present in the wrong amounts, or the diversity of species is lower than it should be. This can disrupt digestion, immunity, and even mood. It is not a single diagnosis but a pattern that underlies or contributes to many gut and systemic problems.
sections.detailed_summary:
The human gut normally hosts trillions of microorganisms — bacteria, viruses, fungi, and archaea — that collectively form the gut microbiome. In a healthy state, these microbes help digest food, produce short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and vitamins, train the immune system, and protect against pathogens. Gut dysbiosis refers to a disruption in this balance: reduced species diversity, overgrowth of potentially harmful species, depletion of beneficial species (such as Lactobacillus or Bifidobacterium), or a shift in microbial metabolite production.

Dysbiosis can occur in different compartments of the gut (small intestine vs. colon) and can involve bacteria, fungi, or archaea. It is not a standalone disease in standard medical classification, but a measurable state that research increasingly links to conditions including irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), obesity, type 2 diabetes, autoimmune diseases, anxiety, and depression.

Causes include antibiotic use, a low-fibre or ultra-processed diet, chronic stress, infections, medications (especially proton pump inhibitors, NSAIDs, and antibiotics), alcohol, and lack of sleep. The gut-brain axis means dysbiosis can affect mental health, and vice versa. Importantly, dysbiosis is often reversible with sustained dietary and lifestyle changes, though some individuals need targeted interventions.

prevalence.summary: Exact prevalence is unknown because there is no single agreed diagnostic test, but dysbiosis is thought to be extremely common in Western populations.
prevalence.estimate: Estimated to be present in a significant subset of people with IBS (40–80%), and broadly relevant to a large proportion of the general population given modern diet and lifestyle patterns.
prevalence.notes: Because dysbiosis is defined relative to a 'healthy' microbiome — which itself varies widely between individuals, populations, ages, and geographies — there is no universally agreed threshold. Prevalence figures depend heavily on how it is defined and measured. Studies using 16S rRNA sequencing show reduced diversity in large portions of Western populations compared to rural or hunter-gatherer populations.

subgroups.name: Colonic dysbiosis
subgroups.description: Imbalance primarily in the large intestine, involving shifts in dominant bacterial species such as reduced Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio or loss of SCFA-producing bacteria.
distinguishing_features: Bloating, gas, and altered stool form primarily after meals with fermentable fibre
Low fecal butyrate on stool testing
Often associated with IBS-C or IBS-D patterns

subgroups.name: Small intestinal dysbiosis (SIBO)
subgroups.description: Overgrowth of bacteria in the small intestine where bacterial counts should normally be very low.
distinguishing_features: Bloating and gas within 60–90 minutes of eating
Positive breath test for hydrogen or methane
May cause malabsorption of nutrients, particularly fat-soluble vitamins and B12

subgroups.name: Fungal dysbiosis (SIFO / Candida overgrowth)
subgroups.description: Overgrowth of fungal species, particularly Candida, in the gut.
distinguishing_features: Associated with oral or vaginal thrush alongside gut symptoms
Elevated D-arabinitol on organic acids testing

subgroups.name: Post-infectious dysbiosis
subgroups.description: Dysbiosis that develops following a gastrointestinal infection, such as gastroenteritis or COVID-19.
distinguishing_features: Clear onset after an acute infection
Reduced Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium on stool analysis

subgroups.name: Antibiotic-associated dysbiosis
subgroups.description: Disruption to the microbiome following antibiotic treatment, which can persist for months to years.
distinguishing_features: May include Clostridioides difficile overgrowth in severe cases
Broad reduction in microbial diversity on sequencing

subgroups.name: Archaea-dominant dysbiosis (methane-associated)
subgroups.description: Overgrowth of methanogenic archaea (particularly Methanobrevibacter smithii), driving high methane production.

symptoms.notes: Often worse after meals, particularly after fermentable carbohydrates (FODMAPs). May worsen over the course of the day.
Increased gas production from microbial fermentation, can be odourless (hydrogen) or foul-smelling (hydrogen sulphide).
Driven by altered bile acid metabolism, reduced SCFA production, or increased intestinal permeability.
Often associated with methane-producing archaea, which slow intestinal transit.
Fluctuating bowel habit is common and may reflect mixed microbial patterns.
Can be diffuse or located around the lower abdomen; often relieved or worsened by passing wind or stool.
More common when dysbiosis affects the upper GI tract or is associated with SIBO.
Dysbiosis can reduce tolerance to foods that were previously well-tolerated, particularly high-fibre or fermented foods.
Linked to reduced nutrient absorption, immune activation, and altered gut-brain signalling.
Mediated via the gut-brain axis; microbial metabolites and inflammatory signals influence cognitive function.
The microbiome influences serotonin, GABA, and other neurotransmitter pathways. This is bidirectional — stress also worsens dysbiosis.
The gut-skin axis connects microbial imbalance to inflammatory skin conditions, though the mechanism is not fully established.

symptoms.symptom: Recurrent oral thrush or vaginal yeast infections
symptoms.notes: Suggests possible fungal dysbiosis or immune suppression linked to microbial imbalance.
Inflammatory signals originating in the gut may contribute to systemic low-grade inflammation affecting joints.
Some bacteria and fungi (especially Candida) may influence cravings by altering appetite-regulating signals.
Dysbiosis can impair nutrient absorption, particularly when the small intestine is involved.

key_patterns: Reduced microbial diversity on stool sequencing combined with GI symptoms
Low levels of SCFA-producing bacteria (Faecalibacterium prausnitzii, Akkermansia muciniphila, Bifidobacterium spp.)
Elevated markers of intestinal inflammation: fecal calprotectin, zonulin
Positive hydrogen or methane breath test suggesting bacterial overgrowth in the small bowel
Elevated candida-related markers (D-arabinitol) on organic acids urine test
Multiple new food intolerances alongside GI symptoms following antibiotic use or infection

at_home.name: Symptom and food diary
at_home.description: Track everything you eat and drink alongside your symptoms (including time of day, stool type using the Bristol Stool Chart, bloating, mood, and energy). Look for patterns: does bloating occur with specific foods, at specific times, or in response to stress? Two weeks of data is usually enough to spot recurring patterns.
at_home.safety_notes: No risk. Stop if tracking increases anxiety or disordered eating tendencies.

at_home.name: Bristol Stool Form Scale self-monitoring
at_home.description: Use the Bristol Stool Chart (easily found online) to record your stool consistency daily for 2 weeks. Types 1–2 suggest constipation (often methane-associated); types 6–7 suggest diarrhoea (often hydrogen-associated or bile acid-related). This helps identify which subtype of dysbiosis may be present.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe to do. If you notice blood in stool, mucus, or sudden unexplained changes, seek medical advice promptly.

at_home.name: Dietary fibre intake assessment
at_home.description: Use a free app (such as Cronometer) to log food for 3–7 days and estimate daily fibre intake. Most Western adults consume 10–15 g/day; the recommended minimum is 25–30 g. Very low fibre reliably reduces microbial diversity. This gives context for whether diet may be a primary driver.
at_home.safety_notes: Increasing fibre rapidly can initially worsen gas and bloating. Increase slowly (2–3 g extra per week).

clinical.name: Comprehensive stool microbiome analysis (16S rRNA / metagenomic sequencing)
clinical.description: DNA-based analysis of stool to identify the species composition and relative abundance of gut microbes. Can detect low diversity, overgrowth of pathobionts, and depletion of beneficial species. Commercial options (e.g. Biomesight, Genova GI Effects, Diagnostic Solutions GI-MAP) vary in methodology and clinical utility. Note: there is currently no universally agreed 'normal' reference range, so results require careful interpretation.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine practitioners, some gastroenterologists, and directly via consumer lab companies. Not routinely ordered on the NHS or most insurance systems.

clinical.name: Lactulose or glucose breath test
clinical.description: Measures exhaled hydrogen and methane after ingestion of a fermentable sugar to detect bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine (SIBO). Elevated hydrogen suggests SIBO; elevated methane suggests methanogen overgrowth (IMO). Sensitivity and specificity are moderate.
clinical.availability: Available through gastroenterologists, some GPs, and private labs. Home breath test kits are available in some countries.

clinical.name: Fecal calprotectin
clinical.description: Measures intestinal inflammation. Elevated levels suggest active inflammation (as in IBD) rather than simple dysbiosis. Useful to rule out inflammatory bowel disease as an underlying cause.
clinical.availability: Widely available through GPs and gastroenterologists. Stool sample sent to a standard pathology lab.

clinical.name: Organic acids test (OAT)
clinical.description: Urine test measuring metabolic byproducts, including microbial metabolites. Elevated D-arabinitol can suggest Candida overgrowth; other markers may indicate dysbiotic bacterial metabolism. Useful but results require specialist interpretation.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine practitioners and private labs (e.g. Great Plains/Mosaic Diagnostics, Genova). Not standard NHS practice.

clinical.name: Zonulin (intestinal permeability marker)
clinical.description: Blood or stool test for zonulin, a protein that regulates tight junctions in the intestinal lining. Elevated zonulin suggests increased intestinal permeability ('leaky gut'), which often accompanies dysbiosis. Methodology is debated and results should be interpreted cautiously.
clinical.availability: Available through private labs. Interpretation is contested; not mainstream clinical practice.

clinical.name: Standard stool culture and microscopy
clinical.description: Detects specific pathogens (bacteria, parasites, Clostridioides difficile). Does not assess overall microbial community but rules out active infections that may be driving or co-existing with dysbiosis.
clinical.availability: Widely available through GPs and hospitals when infection is suspected.

related_conditions.name: SIBO (Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO is a specific form of dysbiosis in which bacteria overgrow in the small intestine. Colonic dysbiosis can contribute to SIBO by altering motility and the microbiome ecosystem.

related_conditions.name: Hydrogen SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Hydrogen-producing bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine is a direct subtype of dysbiosis characterised by hydrogen gas fermentation.

related_conditions.name: Methane SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Overgrowth of methane-producing archaea (intestinal methanogen overgrowth) is a dysbiotic pattern particularly linked to constipation.

related_conditions.name: Hydrogen sulphide SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Overgrowth of hydrogen sulphide-producing bacteria represents a specific dysbiotic state causing foul-smelling gas and diarrhoea-predominant symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Fungal overgrowth (SIFO / Candida overgrowth)
related_conditions.explanation: Bacterial dysbiosis — especially following antibiotics — can allow fungal species like Candida to overgrow, as the bacterial competition that normally keeps fungi in check is reduced.

related_conditions.name: IBD in remission (Crohn's disease / ulcerative colitis)
related_conditions.explanation: Dysbiosis is both a feature and a possible driver of IBD. Reduced microbial diversity and altered Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratios are consistently found in IBD patients, including in remission.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease causes intestinal damage that disrupts the microbiome. Dysbiosis persists in many coeliac patients even on a strict gluten-free diet.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Dysbiosis can impair the gut's ability to degrade histamine (via reduced DAO-producing bacteria) and increase histamine-producing bacteria, worsening histamine intolerance.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: Dysbiosis and increased intestinal permeability can activate mast cells in the gut lining, potentially contributing to or worsening MCAS.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: The gut-brain axis links microbial composition to anxiety. Dysbiosis affects serotonin production, vagal signalling, and neuroinflammation. The relationship is bidirectional — anxiety also worsens dysbiosis via stress hormones.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Reduced Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium, lower SCFA production, and increased gut-derived inflammatory signals are found in depression. Dysbiosis may contribute to, and be worsened by, depressive illness.

related_conditions.name: Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Dysbiosis may increase intestinal permeability, allowing gluten peptides to trigger immune responses and contributing to gluten sensitivity symptoms.

related_conditions.name: Oxalate accumulation
related_conditions.explanation: Oxalobacter formigenes and other oxalate-degrading bacteria are depleted in dysbiosis, leading to increased absorption of dietary oxalates and potential accumulation.

related_conditions.name: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (EPI)
related_conditions.explanation: Insufficient digestive enzymes from EPI lead to undigested food reaching the colon, feeding dysbiotic bacterial overgrowth and altering microbial community composition.

related_conditions.name: Bile acid malabsorption (BAM)
related_conditions.explanation: Dysbiosis alters the bacterial transformation of bile acids, which can worsen bile acid malabsorption and diarrhoea, creating a feedback loop.

related_conditions.name: H. pylori infection
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori infection and its treatment (triple antibiotic therapy) both significantly disrupt the upper GI and broader gut microbiome, contributing to dysbiosis.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: The gut microbiome influences thyroid hormone conversion and immunity. Dysbiosis is associated with both hypothyroidism and autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's). Thyroid dysfunction also affects gut motility, which in turn affects the microbiome.

strategies.name: Increase dietary fibre diversity
strategies.description: Aim for 30+ different plant foods per week (vegetables, fruits, legumes, whole grains, nuts, seeds, herbs, and spices all count). Each plant contains different fibres and polyphenols that feed different microbial species. This is one of the most robustly evidence-based ways to increase microbiome diversity. Start slowly to avoid worsening gas.
strategies.notes: If you have SIBO or severe bloating, high-fibre diets may worsen symptoms short-term. In that case, address SIBO first before aggressively increasing fibre.

strategies.name: Include fermented foods daily
strategies.description: Regular consumption of live fermented foods — plain yoghurt, kefir, sauerkraut, kimchi, miso, tempeh — has been shown in a Stanford RCT (Wastyk et al., 2021) to increase microbiome diversity and reduce inflammatory markers more effectively than a high-fibre diet alone. Aim for 1–2 servings per day.
strategies.notes: People with histamine intolerance may not tolerate fermented foods well. Introduce gradually. Ensure products contain live cultures (check labels).

strategies.name: Reduce ultra-processed foods and added sugar
strategies.description: Ultra-processed foods (UPFs) and excess sugar promote the growth of potentially pathogenic bacteria and reduce microbial diversity. Emulsifiers found in many UPFs (e.g. polysorbate-80, carboxymethylcellulose) directly disrupt the intestinal mucus layer in animal models. Replace UPFs with whole foods where possible.
strategies.notes: Complete elimination is unrealistic for most people. A meaningful reduction has benefit. Focus on swapping rather than restriction language.

strategies.name: Targeted probiotic supplementation
strategies.description: Specific strains have evidence for specific outcomes: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG and Saccharomyces boulardii for antibiotic-associated diarrhoea; Bifidobacterium longum and L. acidophilus for IBS symptoms; L. reuteri for gut motility. Multi-strain probiotics may offer broader benefit for general dysbiosis.
strategies.notes: Probiotics are strain-specific — a product with no research behind its specific strain is of uncertain value. Probiotics are unlikely to permanently colonise the gut but can modulate the environment while taken. Some people with SIBO experience worsening symptoms on probiotics.

strategies.name: Prebiotic supplementation
strategies.description: Prebiotics (inulin, FOS, GOS, partially hydrolysed guar gum, psyllium husk) selectively feed beneficial bacteria. GOS (galacto-oligosaccharides) has evidence for increasing Bifidobacterium. Psyllium husk is well tolerated and supports both constipation and diarrhoea-type symptoms.
strategies.notes: Can significantly worsen bloating and gas in SIBO. Start at very low doses (e.g. half a teaspoon of psyllium) and increase slowly.

strategies.name: Chronic stress management
strategies.description: Chronic psychological stress directly alters gut microbial composition via the HPA axis and sympathetic nervous system. Cortisol and catecholamines affect bacterial gene expression and gut motility. Sustained stress management (therapy, mindfulness, exercise, sleep) is a meaningful lever for dysbiosis.
strategies.notes: Stress management is not a quick fix but a long-term lever. It is frequently underestimated relative to diet interventions.

strategies.name: Regular physical activity
strategies.description: Aerobic exercise increases microbial diversity and SCFA production, independently of diet. Studies show athletes have higher Akkermansia muciniphila and Faecalibacterium prausnitzii — two keystone species. Even 150 minutes of moderate activity per week makes a measurable difference.
strategies.notes: Very high-intensity exercise without adequate recovery can temporarily increase intestinal permeability. Moderate, consistent exercise is the target.

strategies.name: Prioritise sleep quality and duration
strategies.description: The gut microbiome follows a circadian rhythm. Disrupted sleep or shift work is associated with reduced microbial diversity and increased intestinal permeability. Aim for 7–9 hours of consistent-timing sleep.
strategies.notes: Even two nights of partial sleep deprivation measurably alters microbiome composition in human studies.

strategies.name: Targeted antimicrobial treatment (for specific overgrowths)
strategies.description: When a specific overgrowth is identified (e.g. SIBO, Candida, H. pylori), targeted antimicrobial treatment — either prescription antibiotics (rifaximin for SIBO) or antifungals (fluconazole for Candida) — can reset the microbial balance. Herbal antimicrobials (oregano oil, berberine, allicin) are also used and have some evidence for SIBO.
strategies.notes: Antimicrobials should be used for identified targets, not as a blanket approach. Unnecessary antibiotic use worsens dysbiosis. Always pair with probiotic and dietary support to aid recolonisation.

strategies.name: Polyphenol-rich foods
strategies.description: Polyphenols (in berries, dark chocolate, green tea, olive oil, red wine in moderation, colourful vegetables) act as prebiotics for beneficial bacteria. They also have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects against pathobionts. Gut bacteria convert polyphenols into active metabolites, so the benefit is bidirectional.
strategies.notes: No specific dosing recommendations exist. Variety and consistency matter more than any single polyphenol source.

things_to_avoid: Unnecessary antibiotic use — even a single course significantly reduces diversity, and some effects persist for months to years
Chronic proton pump inhibitor (PPI) use without medical indication — PPIs alter upper GI pH, promoting bacterial overgrowth in the small intestine
Heavy or regular alcohol consumption — alcohol is directly toxic to many beneficial bacterial species and disrupts the mucosal barrier
A very low-fibre or no-fibre diet sustained over time — beneficial bacteria starve without fermentable substrate, causing diversity collapse
Repeated courses of broad-spectrum antibiotics without probiotic support — each course further narrows microbial diversity
Overuse of antibacterial soaps and hand sanitisers in home settings — may reduce microbial exposure relevant to immune and gut health (context-dependent)
Excessive refined sugar and ultra-processed food — feeds pathobiont overgrowth and reduces beneficial bacterial populations
Ignoring sleep disruption or chronic stress — both directly suppress beneficial microbial populations and should not be treated as secondary concerns

uncomfortable_truths.truth: There is no single test that definitively diagnoses dysbiosis or tells you exactly what to do about it. Most commercial microbiome tests cannot reliably guide treatment in individual patients.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Patients understandably want objective answers and actionable results. The idea that an expensive sequencing test provides a personalised solution is appealing but currently oversold by the industry.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most probiotic supplements have very limited evidence for general dysbiosis — they do not permanently recolonise the gut and the benefit stops when you stop taking them.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Supplements feel like an active, controllable intervention. They are also heavily marketed. Accepting that dietary and lifestyle changes matter more is harder and slower.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Improving dysbiosis through diet typically takes months to years of sustained change, not days or weeks. Short-term diets do not produce lasting change.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People want rapid relief, and short-term dietary experiments (like 2-week protocols) often produce initial improvement due to symptom reduction from eliminating triggers, not true microbiome restoration.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Stress, sleep, and physical activity affect the microbiome as powerfully as diet does — but these are the least-used levers, even by motivated patients.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Dietary changes feel more specific and controllable. Addressing stress or sleep often requires deeper lifestyle and psychological change that is harder to commit to.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many elimination diets (low-FODMAP, carnivore, very low-carb) improve symptoms by reducing fermentable substrate, but can worsen dysbiosis long-term by starving beneficial bacteria.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Feeling better in the short term is interpreted as evidence the diet is working and should be continued. The downstream microbiome cost is invisible until symptoms shift or worsen months later.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The gut microbiome is genuinely different between individuals, meaning what improves one person's dysbiosis may worsen another's. There is no universal protocol.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Community forums, wellness influencers, and even practitioners often present specific protocols as broadly applicable, which creates false confidence.

key_levers.lever: Sustained dietary diversity — 30+ plant foods per week
key_levers.why_it_matters: Microbial diversity directly mirrors dietary diversity. Each plant food provides unique fibres, polyphenols, and substrates for different microbial species. This is the single most evidence-supported dietary lever for improving microbiome composition.
key_levers.how_to_start: Count your current weekly plant variety for 7 days using a simple tally. Add one new plant food each week — a different vegetable, legume, grain, or herb. Spices count.
key_levers.timeline: Measurable changes in microbiome composition within 4–8 weeks; meaningful shifts in diversity within 3–6 months of sustained change.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Fermented foods introduce live microbes and microbial metabolites that can shift the gut environment. A landmark Stanford trial found fermented food diet outperformed high-fibre diet for increasing diversity in just 10 weeks.
key_levers.how_to_start: Add one fermented food daily: a small pot of plain live yoghurt, a tablespoon of sauerkraut, or 100ml of kefir. Rotate the type over the week.
key_levers.timeline: Changes visible on microbiome testing within 6–10 weeks.

key_levers.lever: Identify and treat specific co-existing conditions (SIBO, H. pylori, Candida)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Dysbiosis driven by an active overgrowth will not fully resolve with diet and lifestyle alone. Treating the overgrowth removes the primary driver and allows recolonisation to succeed.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss with a gastroenterologist or functional medicine practitioner. Request a breath test if SIBO symptoms are prominent, or stool testing if fungal overgrowth is suspected.
key_levers.timeline: Treatment courses typically 2–6 weeks; microbiome stabilisation 3–6 months post-treatment.

key_levers.lever: Protect sleep and manage chronic stress
key_levers.why_it_matters: Cortisol directly suppresses Lactobacillus populations and increases intestinal permeability. Circadian disruption alters microbial rhythm and function. These effects can override dietary gains.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose one: set a consistent sleep time 7 days a week, or introduce 10 minutes of daily downregulation (diaphragmatic breathing, yoga nidra, walking without a phone). Build from there.
key_levers.timeline: Sleep quality improvements affect the microbiome within 2–4 weeks. Stress effects are cumulative and longer-term.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Exercise independently increases microbial diversity and SCFA-producing bacteria, increases gut transit time, and reduces intestinal inflammation — even when diet is unchanged.
key_levers.how_to_start: Aim for 30 minutes of brisk walking 5 days a week as a starting point. Progress to 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week over 4–6 weeks.
key_levers.timeline: Measurable microbiome changes reported in studies after 6 weeks of consistent training.

common_mistakes: Jumping between elimination diets without a structured reintroduction plan — this progressively narrows dietary variety and worsens microbial diversity over time
Treating dysbiosis with supplements alone while maintaining a low-fibre, high-UPF diet — like trying to refill a leaking bucket
Stopping probiotic supplementation the moment symptoms improve, rather than completing a full course and transitioning to dietary sources
Doing one short intervention (a 'gut reset' protocol or a single antibiotic course) and expecting lasting results without addressing underlying diet and lifestyle drivers
Treating every gut symptom as dysbiosis without ruling out other causes — SIBO, coeliac disease, IBD, parasites, and EPI all require specific treatment
Overwhelming the gut with high-dose fibre and probiotics at once, causing a symptom flare and abandoning the approach entirely
Relying on unvalidated consumer microbiome tests to make major dietary decisions without understanding the significant limitations of current interpretation
Neglecting stress and sleep as 'soft' factors — these are physiological drivers of microbiome composition, not optional lifestyle bonuses

specialists.role: Rules out structural and inflammatory causes (IBD, coeliac disease, microscopic colitis), orders breath tests for SIBO, and manages specific overgrowths with prescription antimicrobials.
specialists.notes: Most gastroenterologists work within the framework of diagnosable disease. 'Dysbiosis' as a concept is more commonly used in functional medicine. A gastroenterologist is nonetheless the right first stop to rule out serious conditions.

specialists.role: Takes a systems-based approach to dysbiosis, orders comprehensive stool microbiome testing and organic acids panels, and addresses diet, lifestyle, and root causes together.
specialists.notes: Quality varies widely. Look for practitioners with formal functional medicine training (e.g. IFM-certified). Some tests ordered may lack robust clinical validation — ask for the evidence basis.

specialists.role: Provides evidence-based, individualised dietary guidance for rebuilding microbiome diversity without unnecessarily restricting the diet. Can guide FODMAP reintroduction, fibre rebuilding, and fermented food integration.
specialists.notes: A gut-specialist dietitian is often the most practically useful practitioner for day-to-day management. Look for someone with SIBO and/or microbiome-focused experience.

specialists.role: Can bridge mainstream medicine and functional approaches, ordering standard tests while also considering dietary and lifestyle factors more holistically than a standard GP appointment allows.
specialists.notes: Availability varies by country. In the UK, some private GPs offer integrative consultations. In Australia and the US, integrative medicine practitioners are more widely available.

specialists.role: Addresses the bidirectional gut-brain relationship through gut-directed hypnotherapy or CBT, which have strong evidence for IBS and dysbiosis-driven symptoms.
specialists.notes: Gut-directed hypnotherapy (e.g. Manchester Protocol) has RCT evidence for IBS. Worth considering alongside physical interventions, not just as a last resort.

patient_communities.name: r/Microbiome
patient_communities.notes: Active community sharing experiences with stool testing, dietary strategies, and supplements. Good for peer support and navigating testing options, but verify any medical claims independently.

patient_communities.name: r/SIBO
patient_communities.notes: Focused on SIBO but highly relevant to dysbiosis more broadly. Contains detailed patient experiences with breath testing, treatments, and dietary approaches.

patient_communities.name: r/GutHealth
patient_communities.notes: Broader gut health community. Good for general discussion and finding practitioners. Quality of information is mixed — use critically.

patient_communities.name: The Gut Health Community (Facebook group)
patient_communities.notes: Multiple groups under this name. Look for groups moderated by dietitians or healthcare practitioners for more reliable information.

learning_resources.title: The Good Gut
learning_resources.description: Written by Stanford microbiome researchers, this is one of the most evidence-grounded lay books on the gut microbiome. Covers how diet shapes microbial communities and what this means for health. Highly accessible and scientifically credible.

learning_resources.title: Fiber-fueled
learning_resources.description: A gastroenterologist's practical guide to improving microbiome health through plant diversity. Strong on practical dietary strategies and the science of fermented foods. Optimistic in tone — useful counterbalance to elimination-diet culture.

learning_resources.title: Gut Check
learning_resources.description: Covers the relationship between the microbiome and chronic disease. Note: Gundry's lectin-restriction recommendations are controversial and not mainstream evidence-based — read critically, taking useful frameworks while noting contested claims.

learning_resources.title: Diet-induced alterations in gut microflora contribute to lethal pulmonary damage in TLR2/TLR4-deficient mice (Sonnenburg et al., Cell 2016 — Microbiota-accessible carbohydrates paper)
learning_resources.description: Landmark paper demonstrating that low-fibre diets reduce microbiome diversity across generations. Foundational for understanding why dietary fibre is so critical for microbial health.

learning_resources.title: Gut Microbiota for Health (website)
learning_resources.author_or_source: European Society for Neurogastroenterology and Motility
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based articles on microbiome science written for both patients and healthcare professionals. Covers dysbiosis, diet, probiotics, and disease links. Reliable, non-commercial source.

learning_resources.title: Zoe Science & Nutrition Podcast
learning_resources.description: Tim Spector is one of the world's leading microbiome researchers. The podcast covers microbiome science, diet, and metabolic health with good scientific rigour. Episodes on fermented foods, dietary diversity, and dysbiosis are particularly relevant.

learning_resources.title: Human gut microbiota in health and disease (review)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Jandhyala et al., World Journal of Gastroenterology, 2015
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive, freely accessible review of the gut microbiome in health and disease, covering composition, function, dysbiosis mechanisms, and clinical links. Good scientific foundation for those wanting to understand the evidence base.

sections.relevance_signal: **Chronic, low-grade gut symptoms** without a clear trigger. Worsened by antibiotics. Improved somewhat by probiotics. No single food trigger but general instability.
